text: Finally get to whiteboard with my team again instead of squinting at a shared screen — this is a win.
Three days feels like a reasonable middle ground. Not thrilled, not mad.
The onboarding buddies for our new hires will actually be in the room now, which matters more than people realize.
Honestly my best ideas come from overhearing the design team argue two desks away. Missed that.
I worry the open floor is going to be a wall of noise on the days everyone overlaps.
Culture isn't a Slack channel. Glad leadership seems to get that.
Can we please sort out meeting rooms before Tuesday? There are never enough as it is.
Mentoring my junior over video never clicked. In person she just turns her chair around and asks. Way better.
I'm cautiously optimistic. Ask me again after a month of commuting.
The energy in the office on a full day is something you can't fake remotely.
Give it a quarter and the parking situation will tell us if three days was realistic.
My earliest train is the one that's least dependable, so on in-office mornings I can't always count on arriving when I'm supposed to.
Spontaneous coffee-line conversations solve problems that would take a week of emails. I'm in.
Focus is my concern. My deep-work blocks are the whole reason my remote days were productive.
Newer folks especially need this — you learn the unwritten rules by being around people.
Three days in, two days home strikes me as the sweet spot for collaboration without burning everyone out.
I like it. The team feels like a team again and not a grid of thumbnails.
Please add more phone booths. Taking calls at your desk in an open plan is misery for everyone nearby.
The mentorship angle sells it for me. My best growth came from sitting next to someone senior.
Morale on our squad has been flat; being together might actually help with that.
I get the collaboration argument, I just hope it doesn't become five days by stealth.
Onboarding a remote hire last year was painful. This should make the next one much smoother.
Meeting rooms are going to be the bottleneck. Watch this space.
I do my clearest thinking in a quiet house, so I'm bracing for the noise adjustment.
Culture-wise, this is the right call. You can feel when a team's never in the same room.
Three days is fine. I just want predictability so I can plan the rest of my week.
The apprenticeship-style learning that happens in person is genuinely hard to replicate on a call.
I'll miss the quiet, but I won't miss feeling disconnected from everyone.
Cross-team collaboration has been our weak spot. Being co-located a few days should help.
Whoever books all the rooms for recurring syncs, please free some up for the rest of us.
Excited to actually eat lunch with coworkers instead of alone at my kitchen table.
The noise level worries me more than the commute, honestly.
Good for junior staff, neutral for me, but I'll make it work.
Being visible to leadership in person probably helps careers, like it or not.
I think morale gets a real bump from just seeing faces again.
Hoping we get a desk-booking system that actually works, or seating will be chaos.
My mentee learns faster when she can watch me work through a problem live.
Collaboration is the obvious upside; the open-plan noise is the obvious downside. Net positive, I think.
My care responsibilities change from one week to the next, so a fixed set of in-office days is hard for me to commit to consistently.
I'm a fan. Remote was efficient but a little lonely.
The real test is whether we protect some quiet space for focus work.
New hires ghosting after two months was partly an onboarding problem. This should help retention.
Team culture is built in the small moments between meetings, and you only get those in person.
Three days, sure, but I'd trade a fixed schedule for the flexibility to pick which three.
I already know Wednesdays will be a fight for conference rooms.
Genuinely looking forward to mentoring in person again. Screen-sharing just isn't the same.
My productivity lives and dies by uninterrupted focus time, so I'll be hunting for a quiet corner.
This helps the newer team members way more than the veterans, and that's a good thing.
Morale, collaboration, all the soft stuff — it's real, and it's better face to face.
Please, more small rooms for one-on-ones. The big rooms sit empty while we can't find a huddle space.
I like seeing my team, I just don't love the drive. Small price, I guess.
Onboarding will be night and day compared to doing it fully remote.
Overhearing how other people solve things is underrated. You can't Slack your way into that.
Noise-cancelling headphones are about to become the most important perk in the building.
Three days feels sustainable. Five would not have.
The culture argument is the strongest one here, in my view.
Mentorship works better when it's ambient — quick questions, not scheduled calls.
I hope there's a plan for meeting-room demand, because right now it's a daily scramble.
Being in the room for brainstorms changes the quality of the ideas. I've felt the difference.
Cautious yes. The collaboration is worth the tradeoffs if we manage the noise.
The commute eats a bigger share of take-home pay for people on the lower bands than it does for the rest of us, and that's worth keeping in mind.
I think this is good for team cohesion, which had been slipping.
Deep focus is my worry. Everything else about being in I actually like.
Ramping up new engineers in person is so much faster. Fully support this.
The vibe of a full office is motivating in a way I didn't expect to miss.
Just fix the room booking and I'll have nothing to complain about.
Mentoring, onboarding, culture — the human stuff is the whole point of being together.
I'll take the collaboration boost even if it means noisier days.
Predictable in-office days will actually make it easier to schedule collaborative work.
Newer hires have been struggling to plug in. This gives them a real shot.
My only ask: protect some quiet zones so focus work doesn't die.
Seeing colleagues in person does more for morale than any offsite ever did.
The spontaneous problem-solving is what I've missed most about the office.
Three days is a fair compromise and I'd rather this than a full return.
Room availability is going to be the make-or-break detail here.
Great for mentorship. My growth stalled a bit when everything went remote.
I'm neutral on the commute but positive on being around the team again.
Culture erodes quietly when nobody's ever in the same place. Glad we're addressing it.
Noise is the thing I'll have to solve for. Otherwise I'm on board.
Onboarding the last cohort remotely was rough; this fixes a real problem.
Being physically present for planning sessions just produces better outcomes.
I like the collaboration, I just hope managers respect the two remote days.
More whiteboards and more rooms, please. That's my whole wishlist.
On in-office days I sometimes lose a chunk of working time to lighting and background noise that I don't deal with at home.
Mentoring in person means my junior gets answers in seconds, not hours.
Morale should improve just from the casual reconnecting that happens naturally.
The team-building that happens by accident in an office is hard to design on purpose.
Focus days at home, collaboration days in office — if we hold that line, this works.
New folks learn the culture by osmosis in person. That's the strongest case for it.
I'm looking forward to it, noise and all. The upside outweighs the annoyances.
Meeting rooms fill up by 9am now. Three overlapping days will stress that hard.
Collaboration genuinely improves in person; I've stopped pretending otherwise.
This will make mentorship feel natural again instead of forced onto a calendar.
Morale, onboarding, culture — all the things that were fraying should get some repair.
My concern is purely noise and focus. Everything else I'm fine with.
Being co-located a few days a week should unblock a lot of the cross-team friction.
Onboarding is where this pays off most, and that's exactly where we've been weak.
I'll adjust to the commute. The team connection is worth showing up for.
Just give us enough rooms and quiet corners and this lands well.
On balance I think it's the right move — collaboration and culture are hard to rebuild any other way.
